coredata.dc:title: Two-dimensional ternary chalcogenide nanodots with spatially controlled catalytic activity for bacteria infected wound treatment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Kaili
Ding, Meng
Xiu, Weijun
Zhang, Yu
Dong, Heng
Shan, Jingyang
Wang, Lianhui
coredata.dc:description:
Nanozymes hold great prospects for bacteria-infected wound management, yet the spatial control of their catalytic activity in infected area and normal tissues remains mired by the heterogeneity of tissue microenvironment. Here, we develop a novel two-dimensional ternary chalcogenide nanodots (Cu2MoS4, CMS NDs) with renal clearable ability and controlled catalytic activity for bacteria-infected wound treatment. The two-dimensional CMS NDs (∼4 nm) are prepared by a simple microwave-assisted chemical synthetic route. Our results show that CMS NDs not only have peroxidase-like activity in a pH-dependent manner (pH < 5.5). Based on the generation of hydroxyl radical (
OH) by adding H2O2, CMS NDs show > 2 log bacterial inactivation for both Gram-positive methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) under the acidic condition. Moreover, CMS NDs show good biocompatibility and can be excreted by the kidney in mice. In vivo results display that CMS NDs show good therapeutic effect against bacteria infected wound in the presence of H2O2, but no damage for normal tissues. Taken together, this work provides a renal clearable two-dimensional nanozyme with spatially controlled catalytic activity for the treatment of wounds and bacterial infections on the skin surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-dimensional
Nanozyme
Spatial control
Renal clearable
Wound

Safety and renal clearance of CMS NDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82102335 and 62288102 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2021TQ0218 and 2022M722207 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province-Major Project ( BK20212012 ), “the Belt and Road” Innovation Cooperation Project of Jiangsu (BZ2022011), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191382).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of (a) two-dimensional (2D) CMS NDs were prepared by a chemical synthetic method, which showed (b) renal clearable ability and (c) spatially controlled catalytic activity to regulate ROS generation for wound infection treatment.

(a) Preparation process of CMS NDs. (b) TEM image of CMS NDs. (c) Size distribution of CMS NDs. (d) AFM image of CMS NDs. (e) Thickness distribution of CMS NDs. (f) XRD spectrum of CMS NDs. (g-i) XPS characterization of (g) Cu 2p, (h) Mo 3d, and (i) S 2p orbits of CMS NDs.

(a, b) Peroxidase-like activity of CMS NDs at different (a) pH and (b) concentrations (pH 4). (c) The color change of TMB after incubation with CMS NDs for 10 min (pH 4). (d, e) The steady-state kinetic assay of CMS NDs for (d) H2O2 and (e) TMB. (g, h) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of CMS NDs after reaction with TMB at (g) pH 4 and (h) pH 6.5. (i) XPS spectra of CMS NDs and CMS NDs + TMB + H2O2 after incubation for 10 min under the pH 4 solution.

(a) Fluorescence images of HeLa cells after incubated with CMS NDs and co-stained by calcein-AM (green fluorescence, live) and PI (red fluorescence, dead). (b) Cell viability of human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) after incubated with CMS NDs. (c) TEM images of urine collected from mice after injection of CMS NDs. (d) H&E staining of major organs after treated by saline and CMS NDs at 14th day.

(a) CFU counts of E. coli after exposure to different groups. (b) Bacterial inactivation efficiency of different groups against E. coli. (c) CFU counts of MRSA after exposure to different groups. (d) Bacterial inactivation efficiency of different groups against MRSA. Groups: (i) control, (ii) CMS NDs, (iii) H2O2, and (iv), the final working concentrations for CMS NDs and H2O2 were 100 μg/mL and 100 μM, respectively. (e, f) SEM observation of (e) E. coli and (f) MRSA after exposure to different groups. Data represents mean ± SD, ****p < 0.0001, ***p < 0.001, n = 3.

(a) Wound graphs of different treatment groups. (b) Corresponding wound area changes of mice. (c) CFU of live bacteria in wound tissues after 9 days treatments. Groups: (i) control, (ii) CMS NDs, (iii) H2O2, and (iv) CMS NDs + H2O2. (d) H&E staining images of wound tissues after 9 days treatments. Data represents mean ± SD, ****p < 0.0001, ***p < 0.001, n = 5 in (b), n = 3 in (c).

head.ce:title: Two-dimensional ternary chalcogenide nanodots with spatially controlled catalytic activity for bacteria infected wound treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors, Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors
Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biosensors, Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), Nanjing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Stomatological Hospital, Medicine School of Nanjing University, Nanjing 210008, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing Stomatological Hospital, Medicine School of Nanjing University, Nanjing 210008, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel two-dimensional CMS NDs with spatially controlled catalytic activity is developed to treat bacteria infected wounds on the skin surface.

Nanozymes hold great prospects for bacteria-infected wound management, yet the spatial control of their catalytic activity in infected area and normal tissues remains mired by the heterogeneity of tissue microenvironment. Here, we develop a novel two-dimensional ternary chalcogenide nanodots (Cu2MoS4, CMS NDs) with renal clearable ability and controlled catalytic activity for bacteria-infected wound treatment. The two-dimensional CMS NDs (∼4 nm) are prepared by a simple microwave-assisted chemical synthetic route. Our results show that CMS NDs not only have peroxidase-like activity in a pH-dependent manner (pH < 5.5). Based on the generation of hydroxyl radical (
OH) by adding H2O2, CMS NDs show > 2 log bacterial inactivation for both Gram-positive methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) under the acidic condition. Moreover, CMS NDs show good biocompatibility and can be excreted by the kidney in mice. In vivo results display that CMS NDs show good therapeutic effect against bacteria infected wound in the presence of H2O2, but no damage for normal tissues. Taken together, this work provides a renal clearable two-dimensional nanozyme with spatially controlled catalytic activity for the treatment of wounds and bacterial infections on the skin surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Bacteria infected wounds always happen in human life, which are difficult to heal and life-threatening [1–3]. Nanomaterials with enzyme-mimicking activity, termed as nanozymes, have been applied for treating bacteria infected wounds because of their versatility, ideal stability, and distinctive catalytic properties [4–6]. Notably, some nanozymes such as metal–organic frameworks [7,8], metal compounds [9,10], and carbon-based nanomaterials [11–14], can serve as a peroxidase mimic to produce high reactive
OH by catalyzing the conversion of H2O2 for antibacterial treatment [15,16]. Though their achievements, many current peroxidase-like nanozymes are always active in bacteria infected wounds as well as in normal tissues, while the generated reactive oxygen species (ROS) can kill bacteria to treating wound infection and impact normal cell functions [17,18]. The features objectively hinder their further clinical applications owing to the potential toxicity of peroxidase-like nanozymes. Moreover, the fabrication of biosafe nanozymes with controlled catalytic activity by engineering some biomolecules or nanomaterials are usually complex and high cost [19,20]. Therefore, it still remains challenges to develop peroxidase-like nanozymes with controlled catalytic activity by a simple route.

Here, we prepared two-dimensional CMS NDs with renal clearable and enzyme-like catalytic abilities for bacteria-infected wound treatment on the skin surface. Benefiting from their two-dimensional structure and catalytic activities, CMS NPs have been used in the energy, electrochemical, and biomedical fields [31–33]. However, renal clearable CMS nanozymes with spatially controlled catalytic activity for bacterial killing and wound management, to the best of our knowledge, have never been studied. As-prepared CMS NDs showed peroxidase-like activity in a pH-dependent manner and had the highest catalytic activity for catalyzing H2O2 to generate
OH under the pH 4 condition. In the presence of H2O2, CMS NDs achieved > 2 log bacterial inactivation for both MRSA and E. coli. Additionally, CMS NDs not only showed good biocompatibility for cells and normal organs in mice, but also could be excreted in their intact form against the complicated physiological conditions in mice. Based on these features, CMS NDs showed good therapeutic effect against MRSA infected wound and pressure ulcer. The renal clearable two-dimensional nanozyme with biocompatibility and spatially controlled catalytic activity will be an important discovery for bacteria infected wound treatment.

CMS NDs were synthesized by using the microwave system. A solution: (NH4)2MoS4 (32.5 mg) was dissolved in 500 mL H2O. B solution: CuBr (18 mg) was dispersed in mercaptopropionic acid solution (2.25 mL, 100 mM), then NH3·H2O (0.125 mL) and PVP (30 mg) was mixed with the aqueous solution under sonication for 0.5 h. Then, CMS precursor solution was obtained through mixing A and B solutions, and was irradiated with microwave at 140 °C for 30 min. The products were obtained by the centrifugation (8 k rpm, 10 min) using the millipore (10 k).

We used a microplate reader to evaluate the peroxidase-like ability of CMS NDs. Briefly, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) solution (8 mg/mL, 10 μL) and HAc/NaAc solution (pH = 3, 3.5, 4, 4.5, 5, 5.5, 6, 6.5, or 7, 180 μL) containing CMS NDs (final concentration: 8 μg/mL) were mixed with H2O2 (final concentration: 100 μM, 10 μL) in the 96-well plate. All assays were performed as triplicates. After incubation under 37 °C for 10 min, their absorbance at 652 nm was measured by using the microplate reader.

TMB solution (8 mg/mL, 10 μL), HAc/NaAc solution (pH 4, 180 μL) containing CMS NDs (final concentration: 0, 2, 4, and 8 μg/mL), and H2O2 (100 μM, 10 μL) were mixed in the 96-well plate. After incubation under 37 °C, their absorbance at 652 nm was measured with 2 min interval for 10 min by using the microplate reader. All assays were performed as triplicates.

The steady-state kinetic experiments were performed by adding TMB solution (8 mg/mL, 10 μL), HAc/NaAc solution (pH 4, 180 μL) containing CMS NDs (8 μg/mL), and H2O2 (final concentration: 12, 18, 24, 36, 48, and 58 mM, 10 μL) were mixed in the 96-well plate, or adding TMB solution (final concentration: 127, 242, 485, 970, and 1940 μM, 10 μL), HAc/NaAc solution (pH 4, 180 μL) containing CMS NDs (8 μg/mL), and H2O2 (100 μM, 10 μL) were mixed in the 96-well plate. After incubation for different times, the absorbance of all samples was monitored at 652 nm by using a microplate reader. All assays were performed as triplicates. The kinetics constants were calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation.

We used the electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (EPR) to detect the
OH signal. CMS NDs dispersion (8 μg/mL, pH 4, 0.2 mL) was mixed with H2O2 (0.2 mL) at room temperature for 10 min. The solution was measured immediately after adding 20 µL DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide) by using EPR.

The mice were reared in metabolic cages after intravenous injection of CMS NDs (100 μL, 200 μg/mL). The intravenous injection of saline was used as control. Urine was collected from mice at 12 h and 24 h. The urine samples were directly dripped on a nickel and carbon steel under room temperature, and then characterized by using TEM.

The antimicrobial activities of CMS NDs were studied through the colony-forming unit (CFU) counting method. E. coli or MRSA in NaAc buffer (pH 4, 106 CFU/mL, 100 μL) were treated with i) saline (control, 100 μL), ii) CMS NDs (100 μg/mL, 100 μL), iii) H2O2 solution (100 μM, 100 μL), or iv) CMS NDs (200 μg/mL, 50 μL) + H2O2 (200 μM, 50 μL) at 37 °C for 2 h. All assays were performed as triplicates. Then, the bacterial suspensions were serially diluted with saline and grew at 37 °C to form bacterial colony, then CFU were counted.

All animals were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of the Medical School of Nanjing University. The round wounds (∼6 mm) were constructed and then infected (107 CFU MRSA) on the back of mice (Balb/c, 20 g, female). After 24 h, we randomly divided the mice into 4 treatment groups (n = 5): i) NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4), ii) H2O2 (100 μM, pH 4), iii) CMS NDs (100 μg/mL, pH 4), and iv) CMS + H2O2 (pH 4). The bacteria infected wounds in mice were treated by different groups (20 μL) with 1 day interval for 6 days. After treatments for 9 days, the wounded skin was harvested for histological analysis and counting bacterial CFU [34].

The data in this work were performed based on mean values ± standard deviations (SD). Statistical significance between two groups was analyzed based on the student’s t test using the scientific software (GraphPad).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CMS NDs
ce:section.ce:para: We prepared CMS NDs by mixing (NH4)2MoS4, CuBr, and PVP as the precursors via a microwave-assisted method (Fig. 2 a). The obtained transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image in Fig. 2b and size distribution in Fig. 2c showed that the average lateral size of CMS nanomaterials was ∼ 4 nm. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) result showed the average thickness of CMS nanomaterials was ∼ 1.83 nm (Fig. 2d and 2e), indicating the morphology of as-prepared CMS nanomaterials was two-dimensional NDs [35]. Moreover, Fig. 2f showed the characteristic X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks of CMS NDs at 17.09°, 29.13°, and 47.91°, which can be attributed to the (001), (112), and (220) planes of P-CMS, respectively [36–38]. Additionally, we used X-ray spectroscopy (XPS) to further characterize the compositions of CMS NDs. The characteristic peaks at 952.8 and 932.9 eV were ascribed to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2, respectively (Fig. 2g). Fig. 2h showed that the peaks located at 229.5, 232.7, and 225.7 eV was related to Mo 3d5/2, Mo 3d3/2, and S 2s, respectively. Fig. 2i showed that the binding energy peaks at 162.6 and 161.5 eV were ascribed to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively. All results proved the successful preparation of two-dimensional P-CMS NDs [39].

The peroxidase-mimicking capability of CMS NDs was evaluated through the catalytic oxidation of TMB. Fig. 3 a displayed that CMS NDs achieved the highest catalytic ability against TMB under the pH 4 condition, but they could not catalyze the oxidation of TMB under addition of H2O2 at pH > 5.5, indicating that they catalyzed the oxidation of TMB in the pH-dependent manner. Results in Fig. 3b and 3c further indicated that the peroxidase-like activity of CMS NDs was in the concentration-dependent manner. Moreover, to analyze the catalytic activity of CMS NDs, the Michaelis-Menten constant (KM) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) were determined and shown in Fig. 3d and 3e [40]. The KM of CMS NDs was calculated as 0.39 and 15.10 mM for TMB and H2O2, respectively. Importantly, the Vmax of CMS NDs is 6.55 × 10-8 M/s for H2O2 and 6.04 × 10-8 M/s for TMB, which was comparable with some reported peroxidase-like nanozymes (Table S1). These results suggested that CMS NDs were an efficient peroxidase-like nanozyme and could convert H2O2 to produce
OH.

The
OH could oxidize some cellular components in living body, thus causing the programmed bacterial inactivation [41]. Thus, it was significant to evaluate that
OH was generated by CMS nanozyme. When adding H2O2 into the CMS NDs system, the presence of
OH was monitored by EPR under pH 4 environment, indicating that CMS NDs could convert H2O2 to produce
OH under the acidic condition (Fig. 3f). UV–vis-NIR (ultraviolet–visible-near infrared) absorption spectra of CMS NDs before and after catalytic reaction under the pH 4 solution showed insignificant changes (Fig. 3g). The similar results were shown under the pH 6.5 solution (Fig. 3h). Moreover, XPS spectra in Fig. 3i showed that Cu state in CMS NDs had no obvious changes after the catalytic reaction (pH 4). Results demonstrated that CMS NDs could act as a peroxidase mimic without change of chemical states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety and renal clearance of CMS NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before biomedical application, the stability and biocompatibility of CMS NDs should be considered [42]. Fig. S1 showed the colloidal stability of CMS NDs. CMS NDs showed good dispersion in water, saline, or cell medium after two weeks storage, proving their well stability. The cytotoxicity of CMS NDs was assessed by live/dead staining and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay. In Fig. 4 a and 4b, CMS NDs (160 μg/mL) displayed the low cytotoxicity in vitro against HeLa cells. Moreover, TEM images in Fig. 4c showed that CMS NDs were found in the urine samples from mice after intravenous injection of CMS NDs for both 12 and 24 h, suggesting that CMS NDs had good stability in vivo and could be excreted in the intact form against the complicated physiological environment in mice [43,44]. The toxicity of CMS NDs in vivo was further evaluated by observing the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining images. Fig. 4d showed that CMS NDs caused no significant abnormality of organs in mice. Results indicated that CMS NDs showed the renal clearable ability, good biocompatibility, and potential for antibacterial applications.

We selected E. coli and MRSA as the representative strains to assess the antibacterial effect of CMS NDs under pH 4 NaAc buffer [45]. Fig. 5 a showed that the CFU number of E. coli after treatment of CMS NDs + H2O2 was obviously lower than other control groups. Quantitative CFU counts showed that the bacterial inactivation efficiency of 100 μM H2O2 and 100 μg/mL CMS NDs + 100 μM H2O2 was 1 log and > 2 log for E. coli, respectively (Fig. 5b). Similarly, the CFU number of MRSA after treatment of CMS NDs + H2O2 was lower than other control groups (Fig. 5c), and the bacterial inactivation efficiency of 100 μM H2O2 and 100 μg/mL CMS NDs + 100 μM H2O2 was < 1 log and ∼ 2 log for MRSA, respectively (Fig. 5d). Results indicated that CMS NDs in presence of H2O2 showed higher antibacterial performance than only H2O2, which could be related with the higher reactive
OH generated by peroxidase-like CMS NDs catalysis.

Next, we used scanning electron microscopy (SEM) to observe the bacterial morphology after antibacterial experiments. Fig. 5e showed that the morphology of control E. coli was intact rod, but the morphology of E. coli after incubated by CMS NDs was irregular and disrupted. Similarly, Fig. 5f showed that MRSA exposed to CMS NDs displayed the damaged morphology, while MRSA in NaAc buffer kept the spherical and smooth morphology. Moreover, CMS NDs showed no obvious antibacterial performance under the pH > 5.5 solution (Fig. S2), indicating that CMS NDs were a pH-dependent antibacterial nanozyme.

We validated the in vivo therapeutic effect of the CMS NDs by using MRSA infected wounds on the skin surface of mice as an animal model. No obvious lesions were observed in the CMS NDs + H2O2 group after 9-day treatment; whereas other treatment groups still displayed inflammation and scab in the healing process (Fig. 6 a). On the basis of the wound area collection, the wounds treated by CMS NDs + H2O2 in mice reached to 98% closure after 9 days treatment, but NaAc, CMS NDs, and H2O2 groups only showed 65%, 75%, and 90% of healing rate, respectively (Fig. 6b). Results proved that CMS NDs + H2O2 could efficiently accelerate the wound repair. It was also found that CMS NDs + H2O2 caused ∼ 2 log bacterial inactivation with comparison of the H2O2 group, suggesting their good antibacterial capability in vivo (Fig. 6c and S3). The histological analyses were used to further evaluate wound healing state. Compared to other control groups, no notable scab and lesions were found in the CMS NDs + H2O2 group (Fig. 6d). Though the CMS NDs + H2O2 group could promote wound healing by bacterial killing, CMS NDs were difficult to restore the damaged wound tissue to normal skin, because scar formation was the end result of the wound repair process. Results confirmed that the CMS NDs + H2O2 could kill bacteria in vivo and treat wound infection.

In summary, two-dimensional CMS NDs with renal clearable and spatially controlled enzyme-like catalytic abilities were prepared for bacteria-infected wound treatment. In comparison to the previously reported ternary chalcogenide nanozymes with I-phase structure [27,39,46,47], this work developed a new P-phase ternary chalcogenide nanozymes (CMS NDs) for wound management on the skin surface. CMS NDs showed peroxidase-like activity under the pH 3–5.5 condition, but no obvious catalytic activity under pH > 5.5 environment. Based on the feature, CMS NDs could catalyze H2O2 to produce
OH under pH 4 condition for causing both E. coli and MRSA inactivation. Under the neutral system, CMS NDs showed good biocompatibility for cells and normal organs in mice, and could be excreted in their intact form against complicated physiological conditions. Animal results indicated that CMS NDs could kill MRSA in vivo and promote wound healing. The excessive retention of nanoparticles for long time in wound tissues or body could increase their toxicity [48]. Directly excreting NDs from blood vessel in the skin to kidneys and then to the bladder could avoid their toxicity, because renal clearance was faster than other clearance pathways [48–50]. Our work provides insight into designing nanozymes with spatially controlled activity and renal clearable abilities for the treatment of wounds and skin surface-related infections. In addition, the future focus of this study will further explore the balance between the therapeutic effect and renal clearance required for clinical translation.

Kaili Yang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Meng Ding: Methodology, Data curation. Weijun Xiu: Resources, Methodology. Yu Zhang: Methodology, Data curation. Heng Dong: Methodology, Data curation. Jingyang Shan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Lianhui Wang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82102335 and 62288102), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021TQ0218 and 2022M722207), the Natural Science FoundationofJiangsuProvince-Major Project (BK20212012), “the Belt and Road” Innovation Cooperation Project of Jiangsu (BZ2022011), and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20191382).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.12.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
